# RedwoodSDK: Multiple JSX Routes ## Background A RedwoodSDK project is pre-installed at `/home/user/myapp`. You must add several routes to `src/worker.tsx` that return JSX or `Response` objects directly. ## Requirements Implement the following routes inside the existing `defineApp([...])` call. You must keep the existing `Document` render wrapper so the JSX routes return full HTML. ## Acceptance Criteria * Project path: /home/user/myapp * Start command: npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173 * Port: 5173 * The dev server must boot cleanly without throwing errors. * Routes to implement (all wrapped by the existing `render(Document, ...)` block unless noted): * `GET /ping` returns a JSX element whose rendered HTML contains the text `Pong!` inside an `

` tag. * `GET /about` returns JSX whose rendered HTML contains the text `About RedwoodSDK` inside an `

` tag and a `

` tag whose text contains `React framework for Cloudflare`. * `GET /status` returns a `new Response(JSON.stringify({ ok: true, name: "rwsdk" }), { headers: { "content-type": "application/json" } })` (this route does **not** need to live under `render(Document, ...)`). * `GET /greet/:name` returns JSX whose rendered HTML contains the text `Hello, !` where `` is the URL path parameter. Verify by visiting `/greet/world` and expecting `Hello, world!` in the response body.
Let me start by understanding the project structure and the existing code in `src/worker.tsx`.

todos.content: Read existing worker.tsx to understand current structure
Implement GET /status route (raw Response, no Document wrapper)
Start dev server and verify all routes work

part.text: Let me read the existing worker.tsx file.
Now let me look at the Document component and the Home page to understand the pattern.

output.content:
export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text: Let me look at the `route` and `render` functions to understand how they work, particularly how route handlers receive parameters.

Now I understand the structure. The `render(Document, routes)` function takes an array of routes and wraps them with the Document component. The `route` function takes a path and a handler. For path parameters like `:name`, the handler receives `{ params }` as part of its context.

Let me also check how the route handler receives params - I need to look at how the handler is invoked.

// Use matchPath to check if path matches and extract params
// Merge prefix params with existing params
// Create a new requestInfo with merged params

part.text: I can see that route handlers receive `{ params }` as part of the request info context. Let me check how the handler function is called to understand the exact signature.

output.content:
* - Static: /about, /contact
 * - Parameters: /users/:id, /posts/:postId/edit
 * - Wildcards: /files/\*, /api/\*\/download
 *
 * @example
 * // Static route
 * route("/about", () => <AboutPage />)
 *
 * @example
 * // Route with parameters
 * route("/users/:id", ({ params }) => {
 *   return <UserProfile userId={params.id} />
 * })
 *
 * @example
 * // Route with wildcards
 * route("/files/*", ({ params }) => {
 *   const filePath = params.$0
 *   return <FileViewer path={filePath} />
 * })
 *

part.text:
Now I understand the pattern. Route handlers for parameterized routes receive `{ params }` as the argument. Let me also check how the `/status` route should be handled - it returns a raw `Response` object, so it should be a direct route in the `defineApp` array, not wrapped in `render(Document, ...)`.

Let me also check the example in the llms/rules to see how routes with raw Response are structured.

input.result:
All four routes have been implemented in `/home/user/myapp/src/worker.tsx`: 1. **`GET /ping`** — Returns JSX `

Pong!

` wrapped by `render(Document, ...)` 2. **`GET /about`** — Returns JSX with `

About RedwoodSDK

` and `

React framework for Cloudflare

` wrapped by `render(Document, ...)` 3. **`GET /status`** — Returns `new Response(JSON.stringify({ ok: true, name: "rwsdk" }), { headers: { "content-type": "application/json" } })` directly (no Document wrapper) 4. **`GET /greet/:name`** — Returns JSX `

Hello, {params.name}!

` wrapped by `render(Document, ...)`; visiting `/greet/world` produces `Hello, world!` The dev server boots cleanly and all routes respond correctly on port 5173.
